1: A widow from the sons of the prophets cries out to Elisha: her husband is dead, a creditor is coming to take her two children as slaves. The widow of a prophet — the most vulnerable position in the prophetic community — is the one YHWH addresses through Elisha. The pattern of divine attention to the most marginalized (widow, orphan, stranger — Exodus 22:21-24; Deuteronomy 10:18) is realized in Elisha’s ministry. Jesus’s citation in Luke 4:26 of Elijah’s widow at Zarephath establishes the canonical framework: the prophets’ provision miracles are specifically directed at those whom the social order has failed. The widow’s cry (ṣāʿaq) is the covenantal distress-cry — the same word used of Israel in Egypt (Exodus 3:7).
2: “What can I do for you? Tell me — what do you have in the house?” Elisha begins not with a grand gesture but with a question about existing resources. The one jar of oil (ʾăsûk šemen) is the starting point; YHWH does not create from absolute nothing in these provision miracles but multiplies what the recipient already holds. John 2:6-7: Jesus does not manufacture water from nothing — he transforms water already present in the jars. The principle: bring what you have; YHWH multiplies it. Mark 6:38: “How many loaves do you have? Go and see.”
3: “Go and borrow vessels from all your neighbors — empty containers, and do not get too few.” The only limit on the miracle is the number of vessels. The widow is to gather empty vessels — kēlîm rêqîm — as many as possible. The instruction “do not get too few” sets the terms: the provision will fill exactly as many containers as are brought. The limiting factor is not the supply of oil (YHWH’s provision) but the capacity of the receiver. Jesus: “Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you” (Matthew 7:7) — the asking is the vessel-gathering; the provision is as large as the faith that seeks.
4: Elisha instructs the widow to go inside with her sons, shut the door, and pour. The privacy of the miracle — she and her sons shut in together — protects the sacred act from spectacle. The Incarnation is characterized by this same privacy: born in a manger, announced to shepherds, raised in an obscure village. The provision comes in hiddenness; faith acts in the unseen space before the miracle becomes visible to others.
5: She pours and the oil multiplies as long as there are vessels. The act of faith precedes the visible provision — she pours into the first vessel without seeing the oil multiply; only in the pouring does the miracle occur. Hebrews 11:1: “faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen” — the widow is filling vessels with oil she has not yet seen, acting on the word alone. The multiplication is not instantaneous and complete but incremental, requiring continued active faith.
6: “When the containers were all full, she said to her son, ‘Bring me another vessel.’ He said, ‘There are no more.’ And the oil stopped.” The oil stops precisely when the last vessel is full — not a drop is wasted, not a vessel is left unfilled. The provision is precisely calibrated to the capacity brought. John 6:12: at the feeding of the 5000, Jesus says “gather up the leftover fragments, that nothing may be lost” — the same precision of divine economy. The miracle is not abundance beyond all capacity but sufficiency for every vessel available.
7: “Go, sell the oil and pay your debt. You and your sons can live on the rest.” The miracle has two stages: (1) pay the debt that threatened to enslave the children; (2) live on the remainder. The provision solves the legal problem (debt/slavery) first, then provides for ongoing life. The order mirrors the gospel: Christ’s death pays the debt of sin that held humanity in bondage (Colossians 2:13-14: “having forgiven us all our trespasses, having canceled the record of debt”), and the remaining provision is the life that follows redemption.
8: The Shunammite woman is introduced as wealthy (ʾiššāh gᵉḏôlāh — “great woman”) and hospitable: she pressed Elisha to eat whenever he passed. The wealthy woman who provides for the prophet is the OT hospitality pattern that Jesus will invoke: “Whoever receives a prophet because he is a prophet will receive a prophet’s reward” (Matthew 10:41). The Shunammite’s hospitality to Elisha, though she does not yet know she will need anything from him, is the prerequisite for the miracle she will receive.
9: “I am sure this man who keeps passing by is a holy man of God (ʾîš ʾelōhîm qāḏôš).” The Shunammite identifies Elisha as qāḏôš — set apart, consecrated — through sustained observation of his conduct. The recognition of holiness by a discerning layperson is a significant NT theme: the disciples’ gradual recognition of who Jesus is (Matthew 16:16: “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God”), and Peter’s statement after the miraculous catch of fish: “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord” (Luke 5:8).
10: The roof chamber built for Elisha — bed, table, chair, lamp — is a model of material provision for the servant of YHWH. The four furnishings (bed for rest, table for food, chair for sitting, lamp for light) represent the complete provision of the body’s needs. Matthew 10:10-11: “the laborer deserves his food... whatever house you enter, stay there.” The church’s provision for its ministers has its OT type in the Shunammite’s chamber.
11: Elisha comes and rests in the chamber — the rest of the servant is the fruit of the hospitality. The provision is accepted without excessive formality; the man of God uses what is given for its purpose. The simplicity of the divine servant who accepts provision and uses it appropriately.
12: Elisha speaks to the Shunammite through Gehazi — the intermediary communication. The use of a servant as intermediary reflects the formal distance between a great prophet and a great woman; Elisha maintains appropriate boundaries. The distance is overcome when crisis comes (v27: she grabs Elisha’s feet directly).
13: Elisha asks what can be done for her — offering to use his prophetic connections (“Should I speak a word to the king or to the commander of the army?”). She answers: “I dwell among my own people.” The Shunammite does not ask for status or political access — she is at home where she is. Her contentment with her own people is a form of covenant faithfulness: she is not reaching for more than she has. The one who is content with her inheritance does not grasp; she will receive what she has not grasped.
14: Gehazi identifies the one lack: she has no son, and her husband is old. The barrenness that has gone unspoken — she did not ask; she did not petition; she simply served the prophet. The grace that gives what was not requested. Luke 1:30-31: “Do not be afraid, Mary, for you have found favor with God. And behold, you will conceive in your womb and bear a son.” The Annunciation echoes this structure: the woman who did not ask receives the most significant gift.
15: Elisha has her called and she stands in the doorway. The spatial positioning — she stands at the threshold, between inside (her domestic world) and outside (the prophetic word) — marks the moment of encounter between the ordinary and the extraordinary.
16: “About this time next year you will hold a son in your arms.” The wording echoes Genesis 18:14 almost exactly: kāʿēt ḥayyāh — “when the time revives/at the living time.” The Sarah-Shunammite connection is deliberate: the barren woman who receives a son by prophetic word is the recurring type of the unexpected divine gift of life. The Shunammite’s response — “No, my lord, man of God — do not lie to your servant” — echoes Sarah’s laughter (Genesis 18:12). Both women resist the word before receiving it. The resistance is not disqualifying; YHWH fulfills his promise through the disbelieving recipient as much as the believing one.
17: The Shunammite conceives and bears a son — the word is fulfilled exactly as spoken, at exactly the time specified. The pattern of prophetic fulfillment: the word given is the word accomplished. Isaiah 55:11: “my word... shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose and shall succeed in the thing for which I sent it.” The specific fulfillment of the specific word is the OT’s ongoing demonstration of YHWH’s reliability.
18: The child grows and goes out to the reapers — his life follows the normal course of a child who has been miraculously given. The miracle of origin does not exempt him from the ordinary rhythms of life. Jesus: “he grew and became strong, filled with wisdom” (Luke 2:40) — the miraculously born child lives within the ordinary conditions of creaturely existence.
19: “My head! My head!” — sudden and unexplained illness overtakes the gift-child. The narrative moves from the miracle of birth to the crisis of death with stark economy. The gift of grace is not a guarantee against suffering; the promised child still dies. The greatest promises of God are fulfilled through death, not around it. The typological significance: the miracle child must die before the greater miracle of his restoration.
20: The child sits on his mother’s lap until noon, then dies. The intimacy of the death — in his mother’s arms — and its timing (noon, the middle of the day, the point of maximum light) make the death more stark, not less. The death of the gift-child in the mother’s arms: Mary at the foot of the cross holds a different version of this grief — the promised son who dies in her presence.
21: The mother takes the dead child, lays him on the prophet’s bed, and shuts the door. She places the dead son specifically on Elisha’s bed — the bed provided by her hospitality, now used as the locus of the miracle she will demand. The act is interpretively charged: she is putting the problem where the solution lives. She does not despair; she acts. Her action embodies a theology: the death of the gift-child is a problem for the man of God to solve, not a fate to accept.
22: She sends word to her husband for a servant and donkey — normal domestic preparations — while saying nothing about the child’s death. The reserve is not deception but purposeful silence: she is going to the man of God with her problem; there is nothing her husband can do. The discernment to distinguish what a spouse can provide from what only the prophet can address.
23: “Why go to him today? It is neither a new moon nor the Sabbath.” The husband assumes prophetic visits are only appropriate on designated holy days — the institutional understanding of access to God. The Shunammite’s answer, šālôm — “it is well” — is a statement of faith, not fact: it is not yet well, but she speaks as if it will be. “Do not be anxious about anything” (Philippians 4:6) — her šālôm-answer in the face of crisis is the OT form of the peace that passes understanding.
24: “Drive on and do not slow down unless I tell you.” The urgency of the journey to the man of God in crisis. The same urgency governs the friends who carry their paralyzed companion to Jesus, tearing open the roof (Mark 2:4) — the crisis creates extraordinary measures to reach the one who can address it.
25: Elisha sees her coming from a distance and says to Gehazi: “Look — it is the Shunammite!” The prophetic recognition at a distance — Elisha perceives her before she speaks. Jesus sees Nathanael “under the fig tree” before they meet (John 1:48); he knows the Samaritan woman’s history before she tells him (John 4:17-18). The servant of YHWH perceives what the natural eye does not yet see.
26: “Run to meet her and ask: Is it well with you? With your husband? With the child?” — and she answers each question: šālôm. Three times šālôm. The triple answer of peace in the moment of maximum grief is one of the most theologically dense moments in the narrative. She does not lie; she speaks the eschatological reality she has come to demand. Cf. the risen Jesus’s triple greeting of peace: “Peace be with you” (John 20:19, 21, 26) — in the moment when death has been experienced, peace is the word of the one who has addressed it.
27: She catches Elisha’s feet — the posture of petition and distress (cf. Matthew 28:9: the women clasp Jesus’s feet at the resurrection). Gehazi tries to push her away — the disciple who protects the master from the desperate suppliant (Mark 10:13-14: disciples rebuking those bringing children; Jesus: “Let the children come to me”). Elisha overrules Gehazi: “let her alone, for she is in bitter distress.” The master’s openness to the desperate over the disciple’s gatekeeping.
28: “Did I ask my lord for a son? Did I not say, Do not give me false hope?” The Shunammite holds Elisha to account for the gift he gave. Her accusation is covenantally appropriate: he promised; the promise-child died; he is responsible to complete what he began. The boldness of faith that confronts the prophet with his own word. Cf. the Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:27: “Yes, Lord, yet even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their master’s table”) — the persistent faith that will not accept a no. The prayer that presses God with his own promises is the OT’s model of covenant prayer.
29: Elisha sends Gehazi with the staff to lay on the child’s face. The staff as instrument of prophetic power: Moses’s staff in Exodus; Elijah’s cloak. The proxy mission fails (v31): there was “no sound and no sign of life.” The instrument without the person cannot accomplish what only the personal presence achieves. Gehazi’s failed staff anticipates the teaching that the works of Christ cannot be replicated by those who use his name as a formula without his presence (Matthew 7:22-23: “Lord, Lord, did we not cast out demons in your name... I never knew you”).
30: “As the LORD lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you.” The Shunammite refuses to be satisfied with the proxy; she requires Elisha’s personal presence. Her insistence mirrors Ruth’s oath to Naomi (Ruth 1:16-17) and the disciples’ post-resurrection insistence on the bodily presence of the risen Christ (“Unless I see the mark of the nails,” John 20:25 — Thomas refuses the proxy report). The resurrection requires the personal, bodily presence of the risen one; no messenger or report is sufficient.
31: Gehazi’s staff produces no response — the child neither speaks nor wakes. The failure of the instrument without the person is confirmed. Elisha is told and goes himself. The principle: no technique, formula, or instrument of grace can substitute for the personal presence and action of the giver of life.
32: Elisha arrives and sees the child lying dead on his bed — the same bed where the child was conceived through his prophetic word. The child of the miracle lies dead in the place of the miracle. The connection between promise and fulfillment, between the word of life given and the death that interrupts it, is spatial: the bed is the same. Jesus is buried in a borrowed tomb — the new creation’s first act happens in the place of death.
33: Elisha shuts the door on himself and the child and prays to YHWH. The privacy of the resurrection act — no witnesses — is the pattern of Elijah at Zarephath (1 Kings 17:20) and Jesus at the raising of Jairus’s daughter (Mark 5:40: he puts them all outside). The death and the resurrection are not public spectacle but intimate encounter between the prophet and YHWH, mediated through the body of the dead child.
34: Elisha lies on the child — “mouth to his mouth, eyes to his eyes, hands to his hands” — and the child’s body grows warm. The physical act of superimposition — the living body over the dead one — is a type of the Incarnation’s logic: the living God assumes the full form of human death (Philippians 2:7-8: “emptying himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men... becoming obedient to the point of death”) in order to bring life from within the dead condition. Elijah did the same (1 Kings 17:21); Jesus goes further — not merely touching death from outside but entering it completely. “Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death” (Hebrews 2:14).
35: Elisha rises, walks back and forth, lies over the child again — the child sneezes seven times and opens his eyes. The seven sneezes mark covenantal completeness (seven as the number of covenant perfection); the return of bodily function is total. The resurrection of the dead: not resuscitation but restoration. Hebrews 11:35: “Women received back their dead by resurrection” — the Shunammite is the second in the OT’s resurrection chain (after the Zarephath widow).
36: Elisha calls Gehazi: “Call the Shunammite.” The one who sent her away with a proxy is now the one who calls her to receive back the living child. The circle of the servant’s inadequacy is closed by the master’s completion of the work. “Take up your son” — the return of the gift. Mary Magdalene at the tomb is told: “Go and tell” — the resurrection is announced by the one who witnessed it to the ones who most need to hear it.
37: The Shunammite falls at Elisha’s feet, bows to the ground, takes up her son, and goes out. The triple movement — fall, bow, take — is the physical expression of gratitude for the impossible. The resurrection of her son cost her nothing; she gave hospitality and received life from death. The gospel logic: the gift of life is disproportionate to any human act of service that precedes it.
38: Elisha returns to Gilgal during a famine — the place of Israel’s first encampment in Canaan (Joshua 4:19-20) becomes the place where the sons of the prophets gather and Elisha provides for them. The famine context connects this provision miracle to the Carmel drought provisions of the Elijah cycle and to the wilderness manna: YHWH’s servants do not lack even when the land does.
39: One of the young prophets gathers wild gourds (paqquʿōt śādeh) — an unidentified wild plant, apparently poisonous — and puts them in the stew without knowing their danger. The innocence of the error does not prevent the harm: the stew becomes deadly. Death enters through well-intentioned ignorance. Gen 3: the fatal fruit is taken without full understanding of its cost. The human condition: we do not always know what we are putting into the pot.
40: “Man of God, there is death in the pot!” — the recognition of the deadly ingredient comes only after tasting. The prophet is appealed to immediately; the source of the problem is known but the solution is not. Death that has entered through ignorance requires the prophetic intervention that knowledge alone cannot provide.
41: Elisha throws flour into the pot and declares it safe. The flour is the antidote to the wild gourds — the cultivated grain that neutralizes the wild poison. The flour-type: Elijah’s flour (1 Kings 17:12-14), Elisha’s flour here, the flour of the meal offering — the grain that sustains life in contrast to the wild that destroys it. Jesus: “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35) — the cultivated, provided bread that counters the death that enters through the wild grasping of what was not meant to be eaten.
42: A man from Baal-shalishah brings twenty barley loaves baked from the firstfruits and fresh grain in a sack. The bikkûrîm (firstfruits) offering is given to the man of God — the normal provision for prophets and priests (Numbers 18:12-13). The bringing of firstfruits to the servant of YHWH is an act of covenant faithfulness during the famine: this man has honored the firstfruits obligation even when food is scarce.
43: “How can I set this before a hundred men?” Gehazi’s objection mirrors exactly the disciples’ objection at the feeding of the 5000 (Mark 6:37: “Shall we go and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread?”; John 6:7: “Two hundred denarii worth of bread would not be enough”). The servant’s logic is arithmetic; the prophet’s logic is covenantal. The same word is used: “Give it to the people that they may eat — for thus says the LORD: ‘They shall eat and have some left over.’”
44: “He set it before them. They ate and had some left over, according to the word of the LORD.” The word of YHWH is the operative agent: the multiplication happens through the word, not through any natural process. The three-element structure (set before / ate / had some left over) is exactly replicated in the feeding of the 5000 (Mark 6:42-43: “they all ate and were satisfied; and they took up twelve baskets full of broken pieces”). Jesus is not merely performing a similar miracle — he is consciously enacting and completing the type. John 6:14: “This is indeed the Prophet who is to come into the world!” — the crowd sees the Elisha-type fulfilled in Jesus.
